---
project: webshop
framework: next.js
language: typescript
---

# Project Instructions

## Overview

This is a Next.js 15 e-commerce application with App Router, Server Components, and Drizzle ORM on PostgreSQL. The site serves ~40k daily active visitors at peak and processes roughly 2k orders per day. Stripe handles payments, Resend delivers transactional email, CloudFront + S3 serves product imagery, and the whole thing deploys to Vercel with PostgreSQL and Redis hosted on Railway.

## Architecture

The application follows Next.js 15 conventions with a clear separation between server and client code. Server Components handle data fetching and render the initial HTML, while Client Components are used only for interactive UI elements like cart management, search filters, and form validation. This split minimizes the client-side JavaScript bundle and keeps sensitive operations like database queries and payment processing on the server.

- `app/` — Next.js App Router pages and layouts, organized by route group: `(shop)` for storefront, `(admin)` for the operator dashboard, `(account)` for authenticated customer area
- `components/` — React components co-located with their tests and Storybook stories; split into `components/ui/` for primitives and `components/feature/` for business-specific composites
- `lib/` — Shared utilities including the database client, authentication helpers, Stripe integration, email service, and the feature-flag client
- `drizzle/` — Database schema definitions and migration files generated by drizzle-kit; never edited by hand
- `emails/` — React Email templates rendered server-side, previewed locally with `pnpm email dev`
- `tests/` — Playwright E2E suites organized by user journey: `browse.spec.ts`, `checkout.spec.ts`, `admin.spec.ts`
- `scripts/` — Operational scripts: seed, reset-test-db, generate-types-from-stripe-api

## Data Model

Core entities in the Drizzle schema:

- `products` — SKU-keyed catalog entries with JSONB `variants` for size/color; rows are ~12k
- `cart_items` — session-scoped line items with FK to `products`; ephemeral, purged after 30 days
- `orders` — one row per completed checkout, immutable once `status='paid'`
- `order_items` — FK to `orders` and `products`; price captured at order time, not derived
- `users` — authenticated customers, tied to `sessions` via NextAuth v5
- `addresses` — shipping + billing addresses, soft-deleted when the user removes them so order history stays intact
- `stripe_events` — raw webhook payloads keyed by `event_id` for idempotent processing

Price is always stored in the smallest currency unit (cents for USD, haléř for CZK) as an integer. Never use floats for money. A `prices_cents_non_negative` CHECK constraint guards against negative prices at the DB level.

## Conventions

### Code Style

- Use `const` by default, `let` only when mutation is required
- Prefer named exports over default exports — makes refactoring mechanical and autocomplete reliable
- Components use PascalCase files, utilities use camelCase
- All database queries go through `lib/db.ts`, never import drizzle directly in components
- Server actions live next to the component that triggers them; shared actions go in `lib/actions/`
- Zod schemas for validation live alongside the server action that uses them, not in a shared `schemas/` folder

### Testing

The test strategy has two layers. Unit tests run with Vitest and validate individual functions, hooks, and component rendering in isolation. E2E tests run with Playwright against a local development server with a seeded PostgreSQL database. The E2E tests cover critical user flows: product browsing, cart checkout, order confirmation, admin inventory management, and the Stripe webhook replay flow.

- Unit tests with Vitest, co-located as `*.test.ts` alongside the module they test
- E2E tests with Playwright in `tests/`, using fixtures for authentication and database seeding
- Test the behavior and user-visible output, not internal implementation details or call counts
- Mock external services (Stripe webhooks, transactional email) at the HTTP boundary using MSW
- No snapshot tests — they broke on every Tailwind upgrade and created review noise without catching real bugs

### Git

- Conventional commits: `feat:`, `fix:`, `refactor:`, `test:`, `docs:`, `chore:`
- One logical change per commit; if the subject needs "and", split
- PR titles under 70 characters, lowercase imperative mood with a scope when helpful
- Squash merge to main; the squashed commit takes the PR title verbatim
- Never force-push to main; feature branches force-push is fine

## Authentication & Authorization

NextAuth v5 (`@auth/nextjs`) with the database session strategy backed by the `sessions` table. Three providers are wired: email magic link (Resend), Google OAuth, and GitHub OAuth. Role is a column on the `users` table with values `customer`, `operator`, `admin`. The `(admin)` route group's root layout gates on `role !== 'customer'` via a server-side check in `app/(admin)/layout.tsx`.

Session validation is centralized in `lib/auth.ts` via the `auth()` helper — never roll your own `getServerSession` wrapper.

## Payment Integration

Stripe checkout is implemented via Payment Intents (not the legacy Checkout Sessions API) so the shop UI stays in our domain. The flow is:

1. Client calls the `createPaymentIntent` server action with cart ID
2. Server validates cart, computes total server-side (never trust client-sent amounts), creates the Payment Intent with `idempotency_key = "cart_${cartId}_${cartFingerprint}"`
3. Server returns `client_secret` to client; Stripe Elements handles card entry
4. On confirmation, Stripe posts to `/api/webhooks/stripe`, which must verify the signature via `constructEvent` before any DB write
5. On `payment_intent.succeeded`, mark the order paid and trigger the fulfillment email

Amounts are integers in cents. Currency is always USD for now; CZK support is behind a flag.

## Observability

- Sentry for error tracking, DSN in `SENTRY_DSN`. Sample rate: 0.1 in prod, 1.0 in staging
- Server logs use `pino` with pretty-print in dev and JSON in prod
- Vercel Analytics for Core Web Vitals; budget: LCP < 2.5s, CLS < 0.1, INP < 200ms on p75
- Stripe webhook health monitored via a synthetic test that replays a fixture event every 5 minutes against staging

## Deployment

Vercel deploys every PR to a preview URL, which runs against a branch-scoped PostgreSQL schema created by a `postDeploy` hook. Merging to `main` triggers production deploy. Database migrations run in the Vercel build step (`pnpm drizzle-kit migrate`) — if a migration fails, the deploy fails and the previous version stays live.

Environment variables live in Vercel's dashboard. Secrets are never committed; `.env.local` is for local dev only and is gitignored.

## Performance Budget

- Bundle size: `app/` total JS < 180 KB gzipped per route, enforced by `@next/bundle-analyzer` in CI
- Database queries: < 50ms p95 per route; slow queries log via pg_stat_statements
- Image optimization: all product images served through `next/image` with `sizes` prop set; originals archived to S3 Glacier after 30 days
- Server action p95: < 300ms from request to response

## Security

- CSP: strict; only self + Stripe + Vercel Analytics allowed for scripts
- Rate limiting: Upstash Redis sliding-window, 100 req/min per IP on public routes, 10 req/min on auth endpoints
- Input validation: Zod schemas at every server action boundary, parse or reject — never coerce
- Secret rotation: Stripe keys rotated quarterly, NextAuth secret rotated annually or on breach
- Dependency audits: `pnpm audit --prod` runs in CI and blocks on HIGH or CRITICAL
- Customer data: email and shipping address encrypted at rest via Railway's disk encryption; card data never touches our servers

## Local Development

```bash
pnpm install
pnpm db:up           # starts postgres via docker compose
pnpm drizzle-kit push
pnpm seed            # loads demo products, test users, and a paid order
pnpm dev             # next dev on :3000
pnpm stripe:listen   # forwards webhooks to local via stripe CLI
```

Stripe webhook endpoint needs an HTTPS tunnel for local dev. Use `stripe listen --forward-to localhost:3000/api/webhooks/stripe` — it prints a `whsec_*` secret you paste into `.env.local`.

## Common Tasks

### Adding a New Page

New pages follow a consistent pattern: the page component is a Server Component that fetches data and passes it as props to presentational components. Client Components are only introduced when the page requires interactivity like form input, real-time updates, or client-side state management. Every new page must have at least one E2E test that verifies the page loads with correct data and handles the primary user interaction.

1. Create route in `app/(shop)/[route]/page.tsx` as a Server Component with data fetching
2. Add server-side data fetching using the database client from `lib/db.ts`
3. Create client components in `components/feature/` only if the page requires interactivity, adding `"use client"` directive
4. Add E2E test covering the happy path in `tests/` with proper database seeding for deterministic results
5. If the page is indexable, add OpenGraph metadata via `generateMetadata`

### Database Migrations

```bash
pnpm drizzle-kit generate
pnpm drizzle-kit migrate
```

Never edit generated migration files. If a migration is wrong, create a new one.

Risky operations require extra care:

- `ADD COLUMN NOT NULL` without a default: needs a three-step migration (add nullable → backfill → set NOT NULL)
- `DROP COLUMN` on a table > 1M rows: use `SET UNUSED` first in a prior release, drop in a follow-up
- Adding an index on a large table: use `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY`, which drizzle-kit doesn't emit by default — edit the generated file and add `CONCURRENTLY`, then run `pnpm drizzle-kit push --strict` with a hand-written migration note

### Adding a Stripe Event Handler

1. Append the event type to the webhook switch in `app/api/webhooks/stripe/route.ts`
2. Look up the event in `stripe_events` table before processing; if present, return 200 immediately (idempotent replay)
3. On first sight, insert the raw payload into `stripe_events` within the same transaction as the domain update
4. Add a Playwright fixture under `tests/fixtures/stripe/` capturing a real sample payload

## Release Process

- Tuesday and Thursday are deploy days; Fridays are avoided unless hotfixing
- Release manager (rotating weekly) smoke-tests the preview URL, confirms the Sentry dashboard is clean, and hits "Promote to Production"
- Post-deploy monitoring: watch error rate for 15 min; if > 0.1% above baseline, roll back via Vercel's one-click revert
- Rollback triggers auto-rerun of the prior release's DB migrations — down-migrations must exist for every up-migration shipped
